minicheck_sentence_scores.sentence: The group discussed various aspects of their project, starting with adjustments to the JavaBayes program, including simplifying nodes and identifying cues for inference.
They explored concepts such as "nice walls" and salience, and proposed a model with multiple layers incorporating hidden variables like tour status or hurry level.
The team also clarified distinctions between different node types, including "doing business" and "tourist," and considered adding new values to account for medium hurry status.
To mitigate complexity, they opted for a demo using JavaBayes to test inference capabilities.
Action items included checking how JavaBayes handles new variables and consulting with Srini about its functionality.

The discussion also touched on data collection, including adding navigational tasks with implicit intentions and labeling a corpus to enable analysis, with Fey volunteering as the "wizard" and Liz agreeing to participate in a trial run.
The meeting focused on developing a belief-net system that simulates user financial behavior, influencing decisions made within the network.
The group clarified key concepts, including thriftiness and user finance, and defined how values are updated in the system.
They then discussed determining whether users enter, view, or approach locations based on factors such as ontology, discourse, and user model.
The endpoint decision was also considered, with a suggestion to merge properties into an intermediate structure.
To derive features indicating main actions within places, the team weighed options for primitive entity features versus activity type-derived features.
A new node "underbar-U" was proposed for endpoint decisions based on user models.
The discussion continued to break down complexities in user state and compatibility, encapsulating these as separate entities.
Preconditions for actions were also discussed, including affordability and physical ability.

Finally, a plan was outlined for organizing the project, with tasks assigned to specific individuals and a focus on creating a system that incorporates discourse context into belief-net decision nodes.
The meeting began with a discussion about recording meetings, where it was decided not to synchronize discussions at the same time.
Grad C presented a probabilistic relational model for construal in tourism, which will be updated.
The next meeting will feature Wolfgang Wahlster on the 29th.
Grad C also discussed their generation outputter and preliminary observations on human-computer conversations.
Graduate C plans to reuse slides from Nancy's work on language analysis and matching image schemas.
Their thesis proposal was reviewed, with Professor E suggesting they include scalability of combining constructions.
The group clarified the concept of "out of" in everyday language, agreeing it is a process-oriented term.
They also discussed metonymy and metaphorical language use in human-computer interactions, highlighting the importance of construal in finding relevant information.
Ideas for interactive object conversations were presented, with Professor E offering to provide electronic feedback.
The team began by discussing a software error and noting down details for analysis, while considering new procedures for digit recordings.
A new student with a signal-processing background was mentioned as a potential addition in January.
The discussion then shifted to selecting meetings for data collection, focusing on pre-existing English-language meetings for reliable results.
The group also explored using language models and grammar to analyze corpora, considering alternative options such as recording in other departments or finding a partner on campus.
Equipment needs were discussed, with alternatives to portable devices considered sufficient for some tasks.
The team also addressed challenges of setting up a multi-disciplinary study, including equipment safety and participant engagement.
A proposal was made to give meeting attendees CDs with transcripts after review, and concerns about copyright issues were raised.
The group later focused on archiving old data, clearing space, and acquiring additional storage capacity.
Disk space issues were resolved by moving data from the Meeting Recorder to the Broadcast News disk, freeing up space.
PhD Candidate proposed studying overlapping speech zones using a cluster algorithm or neural net.
Concerns were raised about using neural networks due to difficulty in understanding internal workings, with Professor D suggesting sensitivity analyses and normalizing inputs.
The team discussed microphone arrays for noise reduction and noise cancellation, as well as the challenges of using multiple microphones in conference settings.
An action item was proposed to discuss anonymization of data at the next meeting.
The meeting discussed various topics related to recording, transcription, and corpus management.
It was suggested that the agenda-making process be revised to give attendees a better idea of topics to be discussed.
A graduate student volunteered to send out reminders for agenda items and will look into ordering wireless microphones to replace some of the wired ones.
The group also discussed transcription progress, with about 32-35 hours of recordings already completed.
Postdoc F plans to clean and channelize the data, while PhD B suggested using type token frequency listings to identify inconsistencies in transcriptions.
The team is working on bleep editing and sharing transcripts, including providing a web interface for participants to review and submit exclusion requests.
A proposed system was discussed for distributing meeting transcripts, with some attendees questioning the need for electronic access and others advocating for editing capabilities.
The group decided that edited transcripts should be accessible via a website, allowing users to print them if needed, but not via email due to privacy concerns.

Finally, the discussion centered on presenting agreements in a printed form, with the group agreeing to consider including transcripts, audio or video recordings, and revising notes to reflect a preference for using all available data.
The meeting focused on various tasks related to the DARPA meeting in mid-July.
The team prioritized improving the transcriber interface and creating a mock-up for question-answering retrieval with a month left before the deadline.
They also discussed moving from manual to automatic segmentations, exploring potential solutions such as retraining models for background noise and using speaker background models.
Issues with current approaches were addressed, including recording numbers and transcription system issues.
The team reviewed data on meeting recognition, with 62 hours of meetings collected and an average of six speakers per meeting.
Concerns about transcript accuracy led to discussions about having participants approve the audio rather than transcripts.
Various projects faced challenges, such as noise in prosodic features and speech interruptions prediction models.
Decisions were made to retrain models with vocal tract length normalization and explore different settings for improved performance.
Improvements were noted in the RASTA filter, increasing performance by 0.8%.
Future experiments will compare systems and approaches, including using principal components on 56 outputs and optimizing features for final word error.
The meeting discussion centered on evaluating a tandem approach using neural networks trained on task data versus broad language databases.
Initial results showed a 10% increase in error rate when transitioning from TI-digits to TIMIT training data.
The team compared performance differences between multilingual and single-language training data, concluding that the difference was not significant.
They decided to run additional tests and consider increasing training complexity to represent variability in the training set.
Various language combinations and features were examined, with a focus on comparing Italian and Spanish performance.
The team also investigated mismatched training and testing scenarios, noticing improved results when using their own normalization methods.
Concerns were raised about the need for large amounts of training data for a hybrid system, but it was decided to retrain the neural network.
The group discussed voice activity detection, neural network performance, and silence detection, with suggestions to try combining outputs or running separate nets for computational advantages.
The meeting began with a discussion about the wireless headset used for the meeting, which had limitations compared to lapel microphones.

The agenda included discussing a kickoff meeting, progress reports, and future plans, as well as PhD F's updates on new compute machines being ordered and the setup of P-make and Customs for running jobs.
PhD F explained how to use "run command" to execute jobs on available machines, including specifying machine attributes for job placement.
The discussion then turned to speech enhancement techniques, with PhD A sharing his work on improving speech enhancement systems by combining LDA with existing methods.

Professor C discussed the addition of endpoint information to the baseline, which improved it by 22%, and PhD D presented related experiments combining features from the French Telecom system with spectral subtraction.
Further analysis was needed to optimize time constants in online normalization.

The meeting continued with discussions on minor changes to results, improvements to signal-to-noise ratio using a combination of techniques, and exploring alternatives for speech enhancement techniques, including spectral subtraction for large vocabulary tasks.
Action items included reviewing literature on speech enhancement applied to large vocabulary tasks and optimizing simple spectral subtraction if necessary.

The meeting began with a discussion about reverberation and noise in recordings, where it was agreed that noise processing is necessary and it's planned to synthesize speech from recorded noise after the preliminary study is completed within 1-2 weeks.
PhD A reported on progress with the Mississippi State recognizer, but issues with feature set adjustments remain unresolved.
The team discussed latency issues in a system, agreeing that 220 milliseconds is too large and that further investigation is needed.
Various solutions were proposed, including optimizing the system with parallel processing, downsampling, and upsampling.
Experiment variations for a neural net were also explored, including retesting configurations with new data and exploring KLT methods to de-correlate features.
The team discussed improving recognition using Macrophone data and considered adding it to the training set for the neural net.
Potential mismatches in noise reduction between training and test sets were also discussed, with suggestions that improving the training set could address issues.
Finally, Professor B will not attend the next two meetings but they will resume on August 13, and an evaluation is tentatively scheduled for November 15 or before December 5-6.
The project team met to develop a remote control that is original, user-friendly, and appeals to a wide market.
They set key goals for the design process, which includes functional, conceptual, and detailed design.
Initially, the team settled on an eagle design for the product and discussed its selling price, deciding on 25 Euros or approximately 17 Pounds.
However, they faced challenges with prototype assembly, highlighting the need to simplify the design.
The team considered integrating all functions into one unit, using menus like those in mobile phones, and accommodating users with varying technical expertise.
They also proposed a dual-function interface with basic buttons and a menu with pictures, but decided to revisit this discussion due to cost implications.
The meeting discussed various aspects of a universal remote control project, aiming to design a device that can control multiple appliances.
Key considerations included power consumption, size, and user-friendliness.
The team explored different design concepts, including a portable device with infrared signals and a flip-top model with main functions on top.
Marketing research revealed users' dissatisfaction with current remote controls and willingness to pay more for better designs.
Product requirements were updated to focus on TV-only functionality and incorporate features such as speech recognition and touch screens.
The team also discussed ergonomic design, potential health risks from repetitive strain injury, and the need for variations in design across different countries or age groups.
Decisions made included removing some features, using rechargeable batteries, and incorporating corporate image into the design.
Marketing will review instruction manuals and questionnaires to inform future decisions.
The meeting focused on designing a remote control with a focus on technological innovation and ease of use.
Initial discussions centered around incorporating trendy designs, such as fruit and vegetable patterns, to appeal to a wider audience.
The team explored various component design options, including solar charging capabilities, which were deemed more viable than standard batteries.
They also considered using a dual power source, but noted potential design implications.
Design aspects, such as customizing the silicone PCB board for smaller switches, power conservation through standby mode, and touch screen interfaces, were discussed in detail.
The team ultimately decided on a black and white LCD screen, rubberised buttons, and a plastic shell with various color options.
Voice recognition was considered but may be too expensive, so alternative methods like whistles or mirrors were proposed instead.
The company logo will be incorporated into the design to identify it as the manufacturer's product.

The team discussed and finalized the design of a remote control, including its infrared transmission method, interface layout, and company logo placement, with evaluation criteria established for features and usability.
A prototype was presented featuring a clam-shell design with a black and white touch screen and customization options.
The team also assessed the product's design using rating criteria, deciding on a scale of 1-7, with an average score ranging from 2.75 to 3.25.
They deemed the spongy feel acceptable and concluded that the button material may minimize repetitive strain injury.
Decisions were made regarding electronics, including chip design, kinetic cells, and casing materials, which will include rubber in the cost.

The team confirmed the price as $12.25, below budget, but costs such as overheads and production expenses are still unknown, potentially requiring a higher product price of up to 50% more if increased.
The team began by discussing their project to create an original and user-friendly remote control.
They brainstormed characteristics of favourite animals using whiteboards to aid in the creative process.
The discussion then shifted to designing a functional remote control, considering features such as a call button, light, and vibration to facilitate location.
Ideas for robust design included a small screen, few buttons, and water-resistance, with potential clip-on and satellite programming capabilities.
However, the team clarified that they do not want to compromise on size or cost.
As the meeting concluded, there was no clear resolution regarding roles within the team, specifically the responsibilities of the User Interface Designer and Industrial Designer.
The meeting centered around redesigning the company's current remote controls based on customer feedback from usability testing.
The marketing team presented a prioritized list of buttons for design based on frequency of use.
The User Interface team then proposed two approaches: one focusing on design and user experience, and another detailing technical functions and components.
After discussion, the team decided to incorporate corporate image and colors, simplify functions, and target teenagers and young professionals as users.
They also agreed to include a power button, numbers, and a single menu button, but omitted VCR capabilities due to international coding differences.

Navigation and control functions were discussed, with suggestions for a scrolling button, separate buttons for volume and channels, and a function switch button for accommodating different devices and modes.
The meeting began by discussing conceptual design for a remote control, considering components such as custom case design and standard parts like buttons and wheels.
A specific design concept featuring a fruit and vegetable theme emerged, with the decision made not to include a screen for navigating multiple channels.
Instead, a graphical interface on a TV screen was proposed.
The discussion continued with consideration of the TV's user interface, including options for accessing settings through a central button or menu.
The team also explored the idea of a touch-based graphical interface for the remote control, which some saw as innovative and trendy but others questioned in terms of feasibility and user need.
Design concepts for the product were then discussed, including ideas for changing colours or designs on the surface, with a squashy ball shape and removable cover proposed.
Prototypes will be created to test user feedback, and options for power sources such as batteries, solar power, or a dynamo were considered.
The Industrial Designer noted that chip-on-print technology may be relatively cheap in bulk, but no concrete figure was mentioned.
The team discussed various aspects of a remote control project, including design and user interface elements.
They decided on using a regular chip for advanced features and evaluated the project's standards using an 11-point criteria table.
The design was critiqued, with suggestions made to improve ergonomics and comfort by redesigning the shape.
A compromise between functionality and design was proposed, featuring a broccoli-like shape with patterns representing different fruits.
Established shapes were retained for the touch pad due to redesign costs.
Overall, the team felt that their product met requirements, fit the budget, and was trendy, and agreed to revisit certain design elements while maintaining creative freedom.
Final reports are to be written.
The remote control project was introduced by the project manager, aiming for a trendy and user-friendly product with a maximum production cost of €12.50 and selling price of €25.
Roles were assigned to team members, including Industrial Designer, User Interface, Marketing, and Project Manager.
The group focused on designing an international remote control, prioritizing simplicity and ease of use at a low cost.
A creative exercise was conducted to stimulate ideas, but no specific details about the design process are mentioned in these summaries.
After some discussion time elapsed, it's confirmed that the project is complete, and further presentation and discussion will occur using the remaining meeting time.
The team discussed designing a remote control with an easy-to-use interface and sophisticated functions, citing Google as an example of achieving balance between the two concepts.
The use of infrared technology was proposed, but potential designs and components were questioned, including the need for a wire.
The discussion then turned to wireless versus wired remotes, with cost savings and practicality weighing against usability concerns.
Market research revealed that 75% of users find current remotes unattractive and would pay more for better designs, leading the team to consider user needs and simplify the design.
A compromise was reached on combining functions into a single button while maintaining recognizability as the Real Reaction brand, and using yellow as the primary colour.
The project will use internet instead of teletext, with each team member having 30 minutes after lunch for individual work sessions.

The project team initially discussed three user interface concepts for the remote control, but concerns about usability and reliability of an intelligent controller led them to shift focus towards a more traditional design.
They also considered language and international compatibility issues, deciding that adaptation mechanisms were necessary without increasing costs or complexity.
As discussions progressed, they prioritized developing a basic, reliable controller with flexibility for users to switch between modalities.

The team eventually agreed on a remote control made from wood, incorporating solar energy as an alternative to battery power, low-level chips, L_C_D_ buttons, and possibly voice control, in order to offer a significant improvement over existing remotes.
The meeting began with presentations from the industrial designer and user interface designer, focusing on the remote control's design, price, and production.
The team discussed various aspects of the design, including color scheme, material, and shape, ultimately deciding to measure its "fancy" feature on a scale from 1-7.
They also debated the usability of the pineapple-shaped remote control, particularly with navigation between channels.
The team decided to use a "coarse to fine" tuning system for channel navigation, opting for option 3.
To reduce production costs below €12.50, the project was conditionally accepted pending further refinement.
The design features a double-curved case made of plastic material and will likely include solar cells, but the team questioned whether one would be sufficient.
A single simple chip will be used in the electronics, with potential voice recognition.

After discussing various components, including the most expensive solar cells, the team approved the design for the Pineapple Control or Flying Saucer product, along with a universal version priced at €25.
The team began by designing an original remote control in three stages: functional design, conceptual design, and detailed design.
They started brainstorming using a whiteboard, initially struggling to draw on paper, but eventually produced decent designs.
Key features discussed included usability for kids and the elderly, infrared technology, and potentially a touchscreen or foldable design.
The team also explored universal remote control functionality.
Next, they focused on the home station's features and functionality, considering price, usability, and aesthetics, deciding that a standard design may be more practical due to testing requirements.
Finally, user requirements were discussed, covering functionality, market standards, and technical specifications, aiming for a 25 Euro price point and €50 million profit goal.
The meeting began with a review of notes from a previous discussion on creating a TV remote control for kids and elderly users, with the consideration of larger buttons for older users.
The marketing team presented findings from an experiment showing that 75% of users find current remotes ugly and 80% would pay more for a fancy one.
They proposed creating a beautiful remote control with good zap buttons and accessibility features.
Design concepts were discussed, prioritizing usability and standardization, including a minimalist design with large buttons and clear icon/text labelling.
The team decided to simplify the remote control by eliminating unnecessary features and using standard buttons, potentially introducing a unique design with different colors or shapes.

They also considered adding visual feedback through LEDs and integrating speech recognition technology, but ultimately opted for simplicity and included backlighting for visibility in dark environments.
The group focused on designing a new remote control with a modern look-and-feel, prioritizing ease of use and technological innovation.
They experimented with various design concepts, considering features such as speech recognition, L_C_D_, and colorful rounded shapes with transparent casings.
The team decided to simplify the layout by moving volume and channel controls to the side and placing basic buttons on top.
Ultimately, they settled on a more rounded shape with backlights and a simple scroll button, using plastic cases with flat buttons due to cost considerations and time constraints.
Although speech recognition was initially considered, it was ultimately deemed too complex and not feasible for the current project.
The team agreed to create five different versions of the remote control, including designs for the logo and docking station, which will be developed further in future meetings.
The project team discussed and refined the design of a new remote control, focusing on its user interface.
They decided on an advanced chip for speech recognition and a simple button layout with color-coded options and text buttons for volume control and page selection.
The design will feature hard plastic buttons, a dark grey colour scheme, and flashing lights.
The team also prioritized usability, ruling out features such as a speaker and spongy texture, and calculated the production cost to be 12.3 Euros.
They acknowledged some mistakes in their previous meetings, including incorrect assumptions about prices and layout issues, and agreed to fill in a questionnaire to improve future collaboration.
Despite some time being spent playing Minesweeper, the team made progress on the project's design and features.
The meeting aimed to kick-start the development of an innovative remote control for televisions.
The project's goals were to be original, trendy, and user-friendly, with a three-stage design process outlined: functional, technical conceptual, and detailed design.
The team discussed using SMARTboard notes for meetings and established an estimated selling price of €25 and target profit of €50 million.
They emphasized the importance of interoperability between devices, including TVs, cell phones, and audio equipment.

User interface options were also explored, with infrared technology proposed to avoid direct pointing at the device, and considerations made for glow-in-the-dark buttons or a display in dark environments.
Finally, the team discussed design limitations due to cost constraints, prioritizing user interface clarity, low power consumption, and features that optimize button lighting based on usage.
The team discussed various aspects of the remote control project, including its features, design, and functionality.
Key goals were to reach a decision on product features and target group within 40 minutes.
Decisions were made to use a base station next to the TV for tracking the remote, with the industrial designer taking responsibility for finding a cost-effective solution.

The team considered implementing advanced functions behind a straightforward interface, including speech recognition, which could be combined with the remote control function to make it easier for users to change channels and find their device.
However, concerns were raised about potential costs and battery lifetime.
The meeting focused on designing a new remote control, prioritizing innovation over ease of use.
Initially, team members were asked to share their notes for the project manager's reports, and they discussed marketing findings and design concepts, including soft materials and colourful designs.
Design preferences included simplicity combined with functionality, and potential shapes for the remote control were suggested.
Various design options were explored, including casings made from different materials and energy sources.
The team decided on a user interface featuring four circular push-buttons and potentially volume or mute buttons.
Limitations in incorporating certain features led to decisions against a titanium casing and advanced chip.
Instead, they opted for regular chips, scroll-wheels, and a sensor/speaker combo.
A single-curved casing with traditional batteries and solar power was chosen, along with a grey and yellow colour scheme.
The meeting focused on refining the product's design and user interface, including decisions such as using a scroll bar as a shortcut and simplifying the casing with plastic or rubber buttons.
However, the high production costs due to expensive components like solar cells raised concerns.
The team ultimately deemed the project too expensive, leading them to consider a more basic design for a different market segment.
The Industrial Designer suggested innovative double-curved case design, but it was not feasible due to solar panel limitations.
Feedback on the remote control's features was mixed, with some participants scoring its design poorly.
Despite the product being of high quality, its price point may be too high, and adding more features could justify the cost.
The team also discussed their experience with SMARTboard technology, citing drawbacks such as slow response time and limited possibilities.
In conclusion, the project was deemed failed due to its limitations, leading to a decision to redesign the product with a new approach.
The committee met to discuss various petitions and issues related to the COVID-19 pandemic.
Petitions were presented on topics such as maternal mental health, cystic fibrosis treatment, family doctor shortages, firearms legislation, Indigenous rights, and environmental concerns.
The government acknowledged gaps in programs like CERB, leaving some individuals without support, particularly single parents and those experiencing homelessness.
To address this, the Prime Minister committed to filling these gaps and providing adequate housing solutions.
Measures were also discussed to combat fraud and tax evasion, including audits and assessments of large employer emergency funding facility applicants.
The government announced a one-time tax-free payment of up to $500 for vulnerable seniors, and assistance for tourism industries, lobster fishery, and small businesses affected by COVID-19.

Additionally, the government committed to working with Indigenous leaders on Wet'suwet'en rights, investing in high-speed Internet and tourism infrastructure, and supporting charities and non-profits during the pandemic.

Concerns were raised about vaccine development and distribution, gun control legislation, treatment of seniors in long-term care facilities, and unequal treatment of industries, which the government addressed through various commitments and measures.

The meeting covered a wide range of topics, including the RCMP's use of law enforcement justification provisions and the implementation of UN Security Council resolutions on women, peace and security.
Petitions were presented on issues such as pollinator protection, the Coastal GasLink project, and the presidential election in Guyana.

The Bloc called for a commission of inquiry into police brutality against aboriginals, while the MP for MissionMatsquiFraser Canyon advocated for addressing internet connectivity deficits between rural and urban Canada.
The Prime Minister denied allegations of withholding funds from the Auditor General's office and offered to work with the new Auditor General to ensure her office has necessary resources.
A government bill aimed at helping people with disabilities was debated, with the NDP pressing for a commitment to extend the CERB for families in need.
Other topics discussed included the government's response to COVID-19, including funding for healthcare, child care investments, and support for small businesses and artists.
The meeting concluded with the announcement of measures to support people with disabilities during the pandemic and the extension of the CERB on July 5.
The discussion focused on reforming the law to remove the defence of reasonable punishment for children.
The current law, which allows physical punishment under section 58 of the Children Act 2004, may no longer be suitable given changing attitudes towards physical punishment among younger people.

Barry Hughes explained that removing this defence would lead to increased police referrals to the Crown Prosecution Service (CPS), who would apply an evidential test and public interest stage before deciding whether to prosecute.
This could result in differences in prosecution between England and Wales, due to varying laws, but the CPS has flexibility to adapt and issue policy guidance accordingly.
Hughes proposed updating guidance to reflect these differences and developing specialist knowledge on the public interest test.
He also emphasized the importance of staff awareness and potential unintended consequences, including malicious reporting against parents and breaching human rights.

A 12-24 month review period was suggested to assess effectiveness, with stakeholder involvement, and it was noted that prosecution numbers for child smacking would likely be small due to available diversionary methods.
The meeting discussed concerns regarding the effectiveness and perception of the Welsh Baccalaureate (Welsh Bac) qualification among employers, learners, and institutions in Wales.

It was noted that the qualification has limited understanding among employers, who often view it as too rigorous and bureaucratic, and not capturing key competencies such as literacy, numeracy, and digital skills.
To address this issue, employer engagement and understanding need to be improved through coordinated approaches linking schools with industry partners.
The initial vision for the Welsh Bac focused on developing employability skills but has become overly focused on outcomes due to funding priorities.

Colleges vary in their perception of the qualification's rigor, while universities have variable values for it, with some valuing it highly and others preferring four A-levels or considering it an additional qualification.

Experts suggested incorporating industry experts and experiential learning to raise the profile and improve outcomes of the Welsh Bac qualification, which is currently perceived as rigid and intensive in its implementation.
The meeting discussed the rising trend of exclusions in education, attributing it to challenging behaviour and unmet additional learning needs.
Funding challenges and new legislation were identified as contributing factors, particularly for students with additional needs.
Local authorities acknowledged pressure on schools due to budget constraints, potentially leading to increased exclusions.
The panel noted variation in provision between terms and weeks, creating challenges for retaining pupils in mainstream schools.
Collaborative working was seen as essential to provide a continuum of support.
Concerns were raised about quality assurance in alternative provision, particularly home tuition and EOTAS provisions in Wales.
The role of accountability measures in driving exclusions was questioned, and the need for further research on adverse childhood experiences and behavioural issues was emphasized.
Addressing adverse childhood experiences through teacher training and curriculum changes was proposed to improve mental health support and reduce exclusions.
A whole-school approach to emotional well-being and mental health was highlighted as crucial in preventing issues from arising, particularly among vulnerable young people.
The Welsh Government's draft curriculum has received broad support but some stakeholders requested simplification and added depth.
While minor changes will be made, its overall concept remains unchanged, with key steps including workshops with subject specialists and publication by January 2020.
The "what matters" statements will be refined from existing feedback to ensure consistency across schools.
Mental health and well-being are mandatory topics, with specific wording in subordinate legislation.

The curriculum aims to empower teachers to deliver lessons that are engaging and relevant, emphasizing the importance of four enabling purposes: strong leadership, excellent teaching, assessment, and accountability.
An accountability regime is being developed to ensure consistency in progression steps across Wales.

The Welsh government has invested £24 million in supporting teachers with professional learning for curriculum implementation, with a national approach focusing on headteachers assessing individual practitioner needs.
The pioneer schools have reported on implementing the curriculum in practice, drawing out themes and issues, while other schools participate through clusters and networks.
A statutory framework will ensure consistency, and a national conversation on qualification reform will begin in November to define qualifications rather than vice versa.
